digest:
fctry is a Claude Code plugin that orchestrates eight specialized agents to enable autonomous software development from experience-first specifications (NLSpec v2). It converts conversational descriptions of user experiences into complete NLSpec v2 documents, generates scenario holdout sets, and manages the build-measure-learn loop until scenario satisfaction is achieved. The system uses experience language for both input and output, with a two-register model for system responses: a technical register for internal agent communication and an experience register for user-facing surfaces. A single operational-voice translation module ensures all user-facing output (build plans, progress updates, error messages, status line, completion reports) speaks plain English, blocking factory-internal vocabulary at the edge.

The core flow begins with `/fctry:init`, where the State Owner assesses the project's current state (greenfield, existing code, etc.) before the Interviewer engages the user in a conversational dialogue to elicit their vision. The Interviewer asks clarifying questions and steers the conversation toward four dimensions: Vision Completeness, Flow Coverage, Edge Case Awareness, and Success Definition, ensuring thoroughness without explicit user prompts. The interview state is saved automatically with explicit uncertainty markers (OPEN, ASSUMED, MISSING) for seamless multi-session resumption via `/fctry:init --resume`. Upon completion, the Scenario Crafter generates scenarios, and the Spec Writer synthesizes the transcript, briefing, and scenarios into `.fctry/spec.md` and `.fctry/scenarios.md`. The system also generates a project synopsis in the spec's frontmatter (short, medium, README descriptions, tech stack, patterns, goals) and seeds the version registry in `.fctry/config.json` with external (0.1.0) and spec (0.1) versions. Evergreen project instructions are created in `CLAUDE.md` at the project root.

`/fctry:evolve <section>` allows targeted spec refinement. The State Owner detects drift between spec and code, and the Interviewer focuses on the specified section, referencing past conversations from global memory. The Spec Writer updates only affected sections, regenerates the synopsis, and auto-increments the spec version. A diff summary shows changes. The viewer's async inbox allows users to drop evolve ideas, references, or feature requests for background processing.

`/fctry:ref <section> <url>` incorporates external inspiration. The Researcher or Visual Translator interprets content (fetching URLs via self-hosted Firecrawl or analyzing designs), and the Spec Writer updates the relevant section and adds an entry to §5.2 (Experience References). `/fctry:ref` without arguments triggers overnight research, presenting analyzed patterns and concrete recommendations (not raw URLs) as actionable cards in the viewer's inbox.

`/fctry:review` performs a deep state assessment, identifying spec-code gaps, guardrail violations, and experience degradations. Findings are categorized by action orientation (Decisions Needed, Ready to Build) and presented with clear recommendations. It also audits CLAUDE.md for drift. An optional top-N mode curates findings by actionability and severity.

`/fctry:execute` drives autonomous builds. The State Owner evaluates scenario satisfaction and provides a briefing. The Executor proposes a plan chunked by scenario impact, considering user-defined execution priorities (speed, token efficiency, reliability). The plan includes a phase type (Capability, Hardening, Refactor, Integration, Polish) and cognitive tier (mechanical, implementation, architecture). Tool permissions are managed via trust tiers (always-allowed, ask-once, always-ask). The system handles failures, retries, and rearchitecting silently, resurfacing only for experience-level questions. Post-chunk verification by the Observer checks scope compliance, structural integrity, and behavioral quality. A mandatory post-build retro extracts lessons into `.fctry/lessons.md`. The build completes with an experience report detailing what the user can now do, not a satisfaction scorecard. Version tags are created and pushed automatically, with release verification gates ensuring tags reach the remote and release artifacts are visible.

The `/fctry:next` command provides instant recommendations by reading cached state (`.fctry/state.json`, `.fctry/inbox.json`), prioritizing incomplete builds, untracked changes, foreman results, inbox items, ready-to-build sections, and convergence order. It offers a single recommended action with a rationale and chains into the command on confirmation.

The live spec viewer provides real-time updates via WebSocket, mission control during builds (visual dependency graph, lifecycle states), a project-centric dashboard, and an async inbox for background processing of evolve ideas, references, and feature requests. It supports dark mode, keyboard navigation, and progressive disclosure. The viewer also includes a memory panel for browsing global cross-session knowledge and a lessons panel for project-specific build learnings.

Hard constraints include operating solely as a Claude Code plugin, using experience language exclusively for user-facing output, maintaining scenario holdout separation, requiring plan-level approval before execution, always consulting the State Owner first, prohibiting human code review, and protecting credentials during autonomous builds.

The system tracks the spec document, scenarios, interview state, changelog, state briefing, visual references, codebase size estimates, execution priorities, build plans, build runs, build checkpoints, version registry, project format version, tool availability, section addressing map, project instructions (CLAUDE.md), project registry, viewer state, verification verdicts, observation reports, build trace, experience questions, phase types, workflow state, spec index (SQLite), section readiness index, section priority, diagram definitions, untracked changes, viewer inbox queue, architecture snapshot, structured tool-output envelope, voice translation module, global reference knowledge base, agent interchange documents, and build lessons.

The system's convergence strategy progresses from core command loop and spec creation to targeted evolution, reference incorporation, and review, followed by plan-gated autonomous building, tool validation, changelog integration, the live spec viewer, and finally, the viewer evolving into a mission control and async inbox surface. Future directions include viewer-as-control-plane, background-worker execution, and cross-project synthesis.

Key anti-patterns avoided include becoming an IDE or project management tool, silently overwriting data, assuming on conflicts, allowing direct spec editing in the viewer, skipping tool validation, drive-by refactoring during feature work, and adopting mutable-edge-adapter patterns.

The system leverages external connections to GitHub, Firecrawl, Context7/DeepWiki MCP, Playwright MCP, Chrome DevTools MCP, Surf, OpenBrowser MCP, Showboat, Peekaboo, and the local viewer HTTP interface for build-time observation and reference fetching. It operates locally on macOS or Linux, requiring Claude Code and internet connectivity for LLM API calls and external reference fetching.

Performance expectations are strict: commands respond within 5 seconds (2 seconds for `/fctry:next`), interviews proceed with 2-5 second agent responses, spec generation within 90 seconds, spec updates within 45 seconds, gap analysis within 30 seconds, build plan proposal within 60 seconds, and viewer rendering within 1 second. Real-time updates appear within 500ms.

The system's core capabilities include progressive disclosure, conversational spec creation, agent pipeline protocol, multi-session continuity, targeted spec evolution, conflict-aware updates, reference interpretation, score-driven harness improvement, automated reference discovery, gap analysis, scenario-driven build planning, goal-gate enforcement, build steering, autonomous execution, build checkpoint and resume, build coordination, convergence milestones, verification, and version registry management.

The system's design principles emphasize experience language everywhere, agent autonomy over implementation details, grounding in reality, plan-gated execution, conversational interaction, progressive refinement, and addressable spec sections.

The system's hard constraints include operating as a Claude Code plugin, using experience language exclusively, maintaining scenario holdout separation, requiring plan-level approval, always consulting the State Owner first, prohibiting human code review, and protecting credentials during autonomous execution.

The system's directory structure places all generated files within `.fctry/`, with `CLAUDE.md` at the project root. Git tracking excludes ephemeral state files via `.fctry/.gitignore`. Migration from older layouts is automatic and silent.

References to prior art and inspirations include StrongDM Software Factory, Spec Markdown, Log4brains, Docsify, Notion's Import UX, CXDB, Leash, Attractor, Claude Context OS, ccproxy, DeepWiki, cc-devflow, CodeBoarding, AntV Infographic, codebase-digest, gitin, greppy, srclight, Surf, Peekaboo, Showboat, agent-skills, agentops, opik, Vibetape, ooda-subagents, claude-code-hooks, darwin-skill, code-smells-skill, Passmark, context+, sem, Dolt, Agor, Dispatch, Zep, and others, all contributing specific patterns or validating existing design choices.fctry is a Claude Code plugin that orchestrates eight specialized agents to enable autonomous software development from experience-first specifications (NLSpec v2).
